column10: The purpose of this support is to help me meet my basic food needs so I can focus fully on my midwifery studies without the distraction of hunger and financial stress. As a self-sponsored student with no family support, I use my limited income for school fees and accommodation. Access to the food bank will allow me to stay healthy, attend classes and clinicals consistently, and complete my training to serve my community as a midwife.
column11:
I believe I qualify because I am a self-sponsored midwifery student with no financial support from home. I cover my school fees, accommodation, and other expenses on my own, which leaves me with very limited resources for food.
At times, I have to choose between buying food and meeting other academic needs, and this affects my ability to concentrate in class and during clinicals. As a midwifery student, my training requires both physical and mental strength, and inconsistent meals make it difficult to meet those demands.
Receiving support from the UTAS SRC foodbank initiative will ensure I have access to regular meals, stay healthy, and focus fully on my studies. My goal is to complete my training and serve my community as a compassionate midwife, and this support will directly help me reach that goal.
column12:
Currently, I am a self-sponsored student with no financial support from my family. I rely entirely on my own income to pay for school fees, accommodation, books, and other academic expenses. After covering these essential costs, I am left with very little to buy food regularly.
As a midwifery student, I also spend long hours in classes and clinicals, which makes it hard to take on regular part-time work. This situation makes it difficult to afford consistent meals, and sometimes I go without food so I can meet my other school obligations.
column13:
Limited access to food affects both my studies and daily life on campus. When I don’t eat regularly, I feel weak, tired, and find it hard to concentrate during lectures and clinicals. Midwifery training is physically and mentally demanding, so going to class or clinical practice hungry makes it difficult to perform at my best.
It also affects my daily life because I spend a lot of time worrying about where my next meal will come from instead of focusing on my studies and assignments. Sometimes I have to skip meals to save money for school fees and materials, which leaves me exhausted and less motivated.
Access to regular meals would help me stay healthy, focus better, and participate fully in both my academic and clinical work.

column10:
My purpose is not merely to receive free food, but to secure the basic nutritional foundation I need to function effectively as a student. Adequate feeding is essential to learning, and without it, my academic potential is significantly undermined.
Specifically, I am applying because I want to:
Stabilize my daily nutrition so that hunger no longer interferes with my studies
Improve my academic focus and performance by ensuring I have the energy and mental clarity needed for lectures, assignments, and examinations
Reduce the stress and anxiety that comes with not knowing where my next meal will come from
Complete my academic program successfully and graduate as a competent professional who can give back to society
I believe every student  deserves equal  opportunity to succeed and food security is the fundamental part of that
column11:
II believe I qualify as a beneficiary of the UTAS SRC Foodbank Initiative because I am a registered student facing genuine financial hardship that affects my ability to meet basic nutritional needs consistently. As a student, my primary financial resources are directed toward academic expenses such as tuition, study materials, and accommodation, leaving limited funds for adequate daily feeding.
There are times when I struggle to afford three meals a day, which directly impacts my concentration, energy levels, and overall academic performance. Food insecurity is a real challenge among students, and this initiative exists precisely to support students like me who are committed to their studies but face economic constraints beyond their control.
I am not seeking this support out of convenience but out of genuine need. I am dedicated to my academic work and believe that with this assistance, I will be better positioned to focus on my studies, perform well, and ultimately contribute meaningfully to society after graduation.
I am truly grateful for this compassionate initiative by the SRC and commit to making the most of the opportunity it provides.
column12:
Limited and Irregular Financial Support
My primary source of income is support from family, which is not always consistent. There are weeks when funds are delayed or unavailable, making it difficult to purchase food regularly.
2. High Cost of Living vs. Student Budget
The rising cost of food and other basic commodities in Ghana means that even when I receive money, it does not stretch far enough to cover adequate meals throughout the week alongside other academic expenses.
3. Academic Expenses Take Priority
Costs such as printing, data, transportation, and study materials often compete with feeding money, and sometimes academics must take priority, leaving little or nothing for food.
column13:
When I have not eaten properly, it becomes very difficult to focus during lectures. I find myself distracted by hunger, unable to follow discussions, and struggling to retain information taught in class.
2. Low Energy and Physical Weakness
Going without adequate meals leaves me feeling weak and fatigued throughout the day. This makes it hard to attend all scheduled classes, participate in group work, or spend long hours studying in the library or at home.
3. Negative Impact on Mental Health
The constant worry about where my next meal will come from creates significant stress and anxiety. This mental burden affects my mood, motivation, and ability to engage positively with peers and lecturers.
4. Reduced Academic Output
Hunger affects my ability to complete assignments on time and prepare adequately for tests and examinations. My best academic work requires full mental alertness, which is impossible when I am not well-fed.
5. Social Withdrawal
I sometimes avoid social situations on campus because of embarrassment around my food situation, which affects my sense of belonging and overall campus experience

This makes it incredibly difficult to concentrate in class, retain medical information, or perform effectively during my clinical duties. Instead of focusing entirely on my training to become a skilled midwife, a large portion of my daily energy is spent worrying about survival, which places an unfair and exhausting emotional toll on my studies.

column13:
First, concentration is very hard. When I haven’t eaten properly – or at all – sitting through a one-hour lecture feels impossible. My stomach hurts, my head feels foggy, and I find myself thinking about food instead of what the lecturer is saying. I have to read the same sentence three or four times before it makes sense.

Second, energy levels are very low. Walking from one class to another feels exhausting. I avoid joining sports or even casual group discussions because I get dizzy easily. I often have to rest my head on the desk during breaks instead of revising.

Third, attendance suffers. On days when I truly have nothing to eat, I sometimes skip class entirely – not because I want to, but because I know I won’t be able to follow anything. I stay in my room to save energy and avoid the shame of sitting in a lecture hall while my stomach growls loudly.

Fourth, my social life is almost zero. Friends invite me to eat together at the cafeteria, but I always say no because I can’t afford even a small meal. I make excuses – ‘I already ate’ or ‘I’m not hungry’ – but inside I am hungry. Over time, people stop inviting you. You become isolated.

Finally, my mental health is affected. I feel anxious all the time – worrying about where my next meal will come from. I feel embarrassed that at my age, I cannot even feed myself properly. Sometimes I cry alone at night. It is hard to study when you feel hopeles
